name: Green Girdle
description: The Green Girdle, also known as the Green Lace or Green Belt, is a significant artifact in the Middle English poem *Sir Gawain and the Green Knight*. Woven from green silk and embroidered with gold, it is presented to Sir Gawain by Lady Bertilak as a token of love and a supposed charm to protect him from harm. Lady Bertilak claims that as long as Gawain wears the girdle, he cannot be killed. Gawain accepts the girdle, valuing his life above the knightly code of honor which dictates he return all gifts. This decision becomes central to the poem's themes of chivalry, temptation, and human fallibility. Gawain's concealment of the girdle from Bertilak (the Green Knight in disguise) during their exchange of winnings reveals his flawed nature and fear of death. He ultimately confesses his transgression to the Green Knight, who lightly wounds him as penance for his dishonesty. The Green Girdle then becomes a symbol of Gawain's shame and a reminder of his human frailty. He wears it as a constant reminder of his failure, and the other knights of the Round Table adopt it as a baldric in solidarity with Gawain, transforming a symbol of shame into one of honor and fellowship. The girdle represents the complex interplay between courtly love, knightly ideals, and the very real human desire for self-preservation. Its power lies not in any inherent magical property, but in its ability to expose Gawain's vulnerabilities and ultimately lead to his moral growth.
subtitle: Token of protection, symbol of shame.
currentLocation: Unknown. Exists primarily within the narrative of *Sir Gawain and the Green Knight*.

keyMyths.title: The Temptation
keyMyths.description: Lady Bertilak attempts to seduce Gawain and offers him the Green Girdle as a protection against harm, testing his chivalry and honor.

keyMyths.title: The Confession
keyMyths.description: Gawain confesses to the Green Knight that he concealed the girdle, fearing for his life. The Green Knight wounds him slightly as penance.
